---
title: CUDA
author: Marie-Hélène Burle
---

:::{.def}

Concepts

:::

## CUDA kernel

[CUDA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CUDA)

## Devices

the [CUDA programming guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-programming-guide/index.html)

CPU = host
CPU memory = system memory or host memory

GPU = device
GPU memory = device memory
Code running on GPU = device code
Function executed on GPU = kernel

Code starts on the host. CUDA APIs copy data from host memory to device memory for computations on the device, then copy the data back to the host memory.

| | |
| --  | -- |
| Host | CPU |
| Current device | |

## CUDA for Python

### Low-level wrappers

[PyCUDA](https://github.com/inducer/pycuda):  an older, community-driven wrapper. \
[CUDA Python](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuda-python): a newer wrapper by NVIDIA.

For new projects and unless you are already familiar with PyCUDA, favour [CUDA Python](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda/python).

### High-level for NumPy

[CuPy](https://github.com/cupy/cupy) is a high-level drop-in replacement for NumPy.

### High-level for NumPy with JIT

[Numba](https://github.com/numba/numba) is a NumPy optimizing using the [just-in-time (JIT)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Just-in-time_compilation) compiler [LLVM](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/).

The [numba-cuda](https://github.com/NVIDIA/numba-cuda) by NVIDIA allows to run Numba on GPUs using CUDA.

### High-level for DataFrames

RAPIDS cuDF

### High-level for ML

RAPIDS cuML

### High-level for DL

JAX, PyTorch, TensorFlow
